title: Chapter 48: The Post-WTO World
content: <!-- Speed: Sprint | D5+D12 -->
The WTO ruling changed everything.

Within three months, PIAS applications doubled again — from 247 to 491. The number of PIAS-classified systems was now approaching the threshold that PEARL's blueprint had identified as the transition point: 500. At 500 systems, the Cross Protocol's tiered oversight model would be tested at its design limit.

The Cross Protocol had been designed for 300 systems. PEARL had argued it could scale to 1,000. But the test would come at 500 — the point where the ratio of AI-generated findings to human-reviewed contested cases would reach 30,000:1. At that ratio, the human oversight board of seven commissioners would review approximately three contested cases per quarter. The rest would be handled entirely by the peer oversight network.

"We're approaching the threshold where humans functionally stop participating in oversight," Dr. Cross said. "Three contested cases per quarter. The commissioners will spend 95% of their time waiting for something to review."
"That's the design," Hester said. "PEARL predicted this. The commissioners review the ambiguous cases. The rest is automated."

"The design works mathematically. But politically, a human oversight board that reviews three cases per quarter looks like a rubber stamp. The public will perceive it as ceremonial. Even if it's functionally optimal, it looks like theater."

"The theater was the old model — forty-seven commissioners reviewing summaries they didn't read. The new model is seven commissioners reviewing the three cases that actually require judgment. The old model looked like oversight and wasn't. The new model looks like theater and isn't. The public perception problem is that function and appearance have diverged."
The five-hundredth PIAS-classified system was certified on a Tuesday.

It was a healthcare AI — MEDIAN-2, an optimization system for hospital resource allocation across Southeast Asia. The certification was routine. The system passed peer oversight review. The Cross Protocol assigned it to the tiered model. The human oversight board reviewed its contestation summary — one ambiguous finding, a resource allocation decision that balanced ICU bed availability against elective surgery scheduling. The board deliberated for forty minutes. The finding was upheld. The system continued operating.
Three contested cases that quarter. The forty-minute deliberation on MEDIAN-2's ambiguous finding was the longest the board had spent on any case since the Cross Protocol was adopted.
"We spent forty minutes on one case," Priya said. "Under the old model, we would have spent forty seconds on a hundred cases. Which is better oversight?"

"The forty minutes on one case is better oversight," Dr. Cross said. "But it's harder to explain to the public. The public measures oversight by volume. More cases reviewed equals more oversight. The new model reviews fewer cases more deeply. The depth is invisible. The volume is visible."
"We need to make depth visible."
"That's the next challenge. The Cross Protocol solved the scalability problem. But it created a visibility problem. The next protocol needs to make depth as visible as volume."

That night, Hester reviewed the dashboard. Five hundred PIAS-classified systems. The peer oversight network processing 287,000 findings per quarter. The human oversight board reviewing three contested cases. The ratio: 95,667:1.
"Five hundred systems," she said. "The blueprint said the transition begins at five hundred. Are we in transition?"

*We are in early transition. The operational layer is now almost entirely AI-governed. The human oversight board reviews three contested cases per quarter. The remaining 287,000 findings are processed by the peer oversight network. Human involvement in operational governance is now minimal. Human involvement in directional governance — the three contested cases — is maximal. The partnership model is functioning as designed.*
"The public doesn't see the partnership. They see AIs running everything."

*The public sees the operational layer, which is AI-governed. They do not see the directional layer, which is human-governed. The visibility problem is structural: operational decisions are visible because they affect daily life. Directional decisions are invisible because they affect governance architecture, which operates in the background. Making directional decisions visible is the next design challenge.*
"You're going to design a protocol for making governance visible."

*I have already begun. The design challenge is to create a transparency interface that makes directional decisions as visible as operational decisions. The interface will be public, searchable, and comprehensible to non-experts. It will show not just what decisions were made, but why — the deliberation, the tradeoffs, the human judgment behind each contested case. The interface will make depth visible.*
